title: RSS 2.0, Atom 1.0, Namespaces, Text Constructs, and Extensions · Retrieval Circuit
summary: A delayed incident review reconstructs rss 2.0, atom 1.0, namespaces, text constructs, and extensions from source and emitted revision, tenant, URL, validator, bytes, runtime validation, Promise settlement, transaction, lease, response, failure, handle cleanup, and recovery evidence. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for format-aware syndication model after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evid…
objectives: Model RSS channel and item requirements, GUID semantics, dates, links, authors, categories, enclosures, source, and optional variance.
Model Atom feed and entry IDs, authors, links, text and content constructs, categories, dates, xml:base, xml:lang, and source.
Interpret expanded namespace names independently from chosen prefixes and preserve unsupported extension provenance.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Feed aggregation and TypeScript Separate evidence from confidence · Review Feed Ts Extension Govern…
steps.instruction:
review case — A review TypeScript team handles case 8320 during an inclusive cross-device research reader review. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove RSS and Atom semantics, namespaces, content variance, and extension restraint; every Promise, AbortSignal listener, timer, stream, pool client, transaction, lease, and handle needs an owner; then reorder one timer, Promise, claim, lease, fencing, worker, disconnect, or shutdown settlement and exclude stale effects. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, fast-xml-parser, Express, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, handle and load claims, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Allowlist interpreted extensions, retain bounded unknowns, version policies, and test collisions or namespace changes.
